title: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma origin
vignette: 62-year-old smoker with back-radiating epigastric pain, weight loss, jaundice; CT shows a pancreatic mass. The lesion originated from which cell?

clues.text: back-radiating epigastric pain + weight loss + jaundice

clues.note: Localizes to pancreas, excluding biliary tree/liver.

whatMatters: ~85% of pancreatic cancers are ductal adenocarcinoma -> ductal epithelium. Default to ductal unless acinar clues given.
strategy: 'Originated from which cell' = name the tumor, then its default cell of origin (common > rare variant).

eliminations.why: CT shows pancreatic, not biliary, mass.
eliminations.correctIf: CT showed a biliary duct (CBD) mass, not a pancreatic head lesion

eliminations.why: No liver mass/cirrhosis; jaundice is obstructive.
eliminations.correctIf: Patient had cirrhosis, elevated AFP, and a hepatic mass on imaging

eliminations.why: Much rarer; no fat necrosis/high lipase clues.
eliminations.correctIf: Patient had elevated serum lipase and subcutaneous fat necrosis (acinar cell)

eliminations.why: Benign vascular; doesn't cause this picture.
eliminations.correctIf: Imaging showed a multilocular, enhancing vascular lesion (hemangioma on MRI)

reasoning: Back pain + weight loss + jaundice + smoking + pancreatic mass = PDAC
Cell of origin = pancreatic duct epithelium -> D
mechanism: PDAC arises from ductal epithelium with dense desmoplasia; drivers KRAS, CDKN2A, TP53, SMAD4. Markers/associations: CA 19-9, Trousseau migratory thrombophlebitis, Courvoisier sign.

firstAid.location: GI > pancreatic cancer (ductal, risk factors, Courvoisier, Trousseau)

memoryHook: Pancreatic cancer is a DUCT problem — default = Ductal adenocarcinoma.
